name: Master of Science in Management (MSM)

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 31 referenced courses form a broad but coherent management curriculum with functional pillars (finance FIN 600-641, information systems IS 631-688, marketing MRKT 620/645, HR HRM 601, project management EM 636/637 and MGMT 641) and a genuine analytics spine that builds FIN 600 -> FIN 616 -> FIN 620 -> MGMT 735 (Deep Learning in Business), giving real depth (deepestPrereqChain=3).
dimensions.suggestion: Define named concentrations (e.g., business analytics, fintech, project management) so students can navigate the wide elective set with a coherent sequence.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=3 with sensible gating (FIN 600->616->620->MGMT 735; MIS 645->648; IS 663->684) and missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references) indicate a healthy, well-formed prerequisite graph that is neither under- nor over-constrained.
dimensions.suggestion: Tighten a few vague conditions, MGMT 735 lists only 'advanced graduate standing' as an alternative, so a concrete course prerequisite would make the analytics ladder explicit.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data, so the managerial and analytical competencies the MSM develops are not stated despite a rich course list.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish MSM outcomes (e.g., data-driven decision-making, functional-area management, and leadership) mapped to the core courses.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=null leaves the degree total unstated (a master's normally publishes ~30 credits), and with requirementGroups=0 there is no visible core/elective split, so while the 31 uniform 3-credit courses are consistent, the overall credit structure is not transparent.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and separate required core courses from electives so students see how the degree is composed.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present, specific, and readable (e.g., MGMT 735 Deep Learning in Business, DS 644 Introduction to Big Data), but the page is a flat list with no requirement groups, no core/elective labeling, and no stated total, so structure is thin relative to the strong content.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the courses into core and elective/concentration blocks and add a program overview and credit total to make the page navigable.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Currency is excellent: the curriculum surfaces big data (DS 644), deep learning for business (MGMT 735), data mining (MGMT 635), fintech (FIN 611) and financial data analytics (FIN 620), web mining (IS 688), and digital marketing (MRKT 645), squarely reflecting contemporary technology-management practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Stay ahead of the curve by adding a generative-AI/LLM-for-business elective and a data-governance/AI-ethics course to complement the strong analytics offerings.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative across the pool (e.g., MGMT 735 details data-mining/ML for business applications; FIN 620 describes Python-based financial data applications), well above thin/placeholder quality.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are mostly clear with no dangling references (missingCourseCount=0), but several entries carry parsing bleed or embedded prose (IS 631 and IS 684 prereq fields run into descriptive sentences; IS 663 shows a truncated 'Pre or') and MGMT 735's 'advanced graduate standing' is vague, which reduces clarity.

missingTopics.topic: Generative AI and LLM applications for management
missingTopics.rationale: The program has strong ML content (MGMT 735, MGMT 635) but no course on generative-AI tooling now reshaping business workflows.

missingTopics.topic: Data governance, privacy, and AI ethics
missingTopics.rationale: With heavy analytics coverage (DS 644, IS 688, FIN 620), a governance/ethics course is a notable gap for responsible data-driven management.
Beyond IE 659, the core lacks a broad operations-management course, a standard management-master's pillar.
The pool is strong on functional areas and analytics but light on an integrative strategy capstone that ties them together.

emergingTrends.rationale: MGMT 735 and MGMT 635 position students in ML; extending to generative-AI copilots reflects how managers now analyze and act on data.
FIN 611/616/620 already form a fintech-analytics ladder that could be branded as a concentration as the sector grows.
MRKT 645 and IS 688 (web mining) reflect the shift to measurable digital marketing, a differentiator worth deepening.

emergingTrends.trend: AI ethics and responsible data governance
emergingTrends.rationale: As analytics permeates the curriculum, governance and ethics are becoming required managerial competencies the MSM should surface.

topSuggestions: Publish the total credits and a core-versus-elective/concentration structure (currently statedTotalCredits=null and requirementGroups=0).
Define named concentrations (analytics, fintech, project management) to guide students through the 31-course pool.
Add forward-looking electives: generative-AI/LLM for business and data-governance/AI-ethics.
Publish MSM learning outcomes mapped to the core courses.
Clean up a few prerequisite fields (IS 631, IS 663, IS 684, MGMT 735) where prereq text is garbled or vague.
